title: Storms, strong winds in UP claim 56 lives: Man swept away in 80 kmph winds; red alert for heatwave in Rajas... - Bhaskar English
full_text:
104 killed as storm lashes UP:Youth swept away in 80 kmph winds; IMD issues red alert in R’than, heatwaves grip MP
Bhopal/Jaipur/Lucknow/Patna38 minutes ago
The country is witnessing two different weather patterns. In Uttar Pradesh, including Prayagraj and Ayodhya, storms on Wednesday caused around 104 deaths across 30 districts. The highest toll was 21 deaths in Prayagraj and 17 in Bhadohi.
Winds blew at speeds of 80 kmph, and in Bareilly, a young man was reportedly lifted into the air along with a tin shed. Banda recorded a temperature of 45.4°C. A warning for thunderstorms and heavy rain has been issued for 51 districts today.
In Rajasthan, severe heat and heatwave conditions continue. On Wednesday, Jaisalmer remained the hottest city in the country for the fourth consecutive day, recording 46.1°C. The Meteorological Department has issued a red alert for intense heat in Bikaner, Jodhpur, Jaisalmer, and Barmer today, along with a heatwave warning for 13 districts.
In Madhya Pradesh, heatwave conditions persist in more than 25 districts and may continue until May 18. Khajuraho recorded 45.4°C on Wednesday. An orange alert for heatwave and warm night conditions has been issued today for Indore, Ujjain, Dhar, and Ratlam.
Meanwhile, in Haryana, maximum temperatures rose by 1.1°C in the past 24 hours, with temperatures crossing 40°C in five cities, including 42.5°C in Narnaul. A yellow heatwave alert has been issued in the state from May 17.
In Bihar, an orange alert for heavy rain has been issued in seven districts including Purnia and Katihar. In Uttarakhand, a yellow rain alert has been issued for hilly districts today.
Weather conditions for the next two days
15 May:
- Heatwave conditions are also expected in some parts of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Madhya Pradesh.
- Dust storms with strong winds at speeds of 50-60 kmph may occur at some places in Jharkhand.
- Thunderstorms with strong winds and lightning may occur in Bihar, Chhattisgarh, Andhra Pradesh, Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, Meghalaya, Kerala, and Karnataka.
May 16:
- Rain and strong winds may occur in Punjab, Haryana, Delhi, Rajasthan and Western UP.
- Hailstorms may occur along with rain in Jammu-Kashmir, Himachal and Uttarakhand. Strong winds may also blow in hilly areas.
- There is an alert for lightning strikes along with rain in Bihar, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Chhattisgarh and northeastern states.
Pictures related to weather from across the country
A tree fell on a car after a storm in Gonda, Uttar Pradesh.
A neem tree fell on a car in Unnao, Uttar Pradesh.
In Mumbai, Maharashtra, children are also going out with umbrellas to protect themselves from the heat.
A man was seen bathing at a roadside tap in Agartala, Tripura.

title: Indore, Ujjain on orange alert as heatwave intensifies across western MP - The New Indian Express
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Madhya Pradesh
full_text:
Severe heatwave conditions have tightened their grip on western Madhya Pradesh, prompting the India Meteorological Department (IMD) to issue an orange alert for Indore, Ujjain and several neighbouring districts.
Residents across the region continued to struggle with unusually high temperatures, with little relief even during the early morning and night hours. Afternoon streets remained largely deserted as temperatures soared.
According to meteorologist HS Pandey, most parts of the state — except the south-eastern region — are expected to witness maximum temperatures between 42 and 45 degrees Celsius over the next couple of days.
Indore recorded a minimum temperature of 30 degrees Celsius on Thursday, which was 5.7 degrees above normal, IMD data showed. This marked the second straight day that the city’s night temperature touched the 30-degree mark. On Wednesday, the commercial capital of Madhya Pradesh had registered a minimum temperature of 30.2 degrees Celsius.
“A large part of western Madhya Pradesh remains under intense heatwave conditions. An orange alert has been issued for the next 24 hours for Indore, Ratlam, Ujjain, Dhar, Dewas and Guna districts,” Pandey said.
He advised residents in the affected districts to remain cautious and take necessary precautions to protect themselves from the extreme heat.
Among other major cities, Jabalpur recorded a minimum temperature of 29.5 degrees Celsius on Thursday, followed by Narmadapuram at 29.4 degrees Celsius and Ujjain at 29.2 degrees Celsius. Bhopal registered 28.9 degrees Celsius, while Guna and Satna both logged 28.7 degrees Celsius. Ratlam recorded 28.6 degrees Celsius and Sagar 27.9 degrees Celsius, according to IMD data.
(With inputs from PTI)

title: Mercury soars in western MP; IMD issues orange alert for Indore, Ujjain - Awaz The Voice
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Madhya Pradesh
full_text:
Indore
Severe heatwave conditions have intensified across western parts of Madhya Pradesh, prompting the India Meteorological Department to issue an orange alert for Indore, Ujjain and several adjoining districts.
Weather officials said most regions of the state, except the south-eastern belt, are likely to record maximum temperatures between 42 and 45 degrees Celsius over the next one to two days.
Residents across western Madhya Pradesh are finding little relief even during mornings and nights, with minimum temperatures staying unusually high and roads in major cities appearing largely deserted during peak afternoon hours.
According to IMD data, Indore recorded a minimum temperature of 30 degrees Celsius on Thursday, which was 5.7 degrees above normal, marking the second consecutive day the city’s night temperature touched the 30-degree mark.
Meteorologist H.S. Pandey said a large part of western Madhya Pradesh remains under severe heat stress, with the orange alert in force for the next 24 hours in Indore, Ratlam, Ujjain, Dhar, Dewas and Guna districts.
He advised residents in the affected areas to remain cautious, avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight and take necessary precautions against heat-related illnesses.
Other cities in the state also recorded elevated night temperatures, with Jabalpur logging 29.5 degrees Celsius, Bhopal 28.9 degrees Celsius, and Ratlam 28.6 degrees Celsius.
The IMD has forecast continued heatwave conditions across parts of western Madhya Pradesh through mid-May, with temperatures in some districts already crossing the 45-degree mark.
